content:
===BEGIN===
It’s easy to think the seasons are caused by Earth moving closer to or farther from the Sun, but that’s actually a misconception. The real reason we experience seasons is because of the tilt of Earth’s axis. Imagine Earth as a spinning top tilted about 23.5 degrees relative to its orbit around the Sun. This tilt means that during different times of the year, either the Northern or Southern Hemisphere leans more toward the Sun.

When your hemisphere leans toward the Sun, sunlight hits it more directly and for longer periods each day, causing warmer temperatures and summer. Half a year later, the same hemisphere tilts away, receiving less direct sunlight and shorter days, which brings colder weather and winter. This explanation is supported by centuries of observations and measurements, so it’s not just hype—it’s basic astronomy explaining what you experience every year.
